entry.rationale: Pennsylvania statewide civil registration did not begin until 1906, so pre-1906 vital events for the Flynn family depend on church records. The Flynns were almost certainly Roman Catholic Irish immigrants in the anthracite coal region. 'Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708–1985' (col. 1401638, ~1.2M indexed records) covers baptisms, marriages, and burials. Searching Thomas Flynn and Patrick Flynn in Schuylkill County may yield Thomas's marriage record — naming his wife, Patrick's currently unknown mother — as well as Patrick's baptism or confirmation and burial entries that corroborate the parent-child relationship.

Dedicated search for Thomas Flynn's marriage to his wife — a required item for any parentage plan. Thomas married ca. 1840–1845 (Patrick born ~1845 in Ireland). The marriage record would directly name Thomas's wife, identifying Patrick's mother, who is wholly unknown to this project. Search 'Pennsylvania, Civil Marriages, 1677–1950' (col. 1571957), 'Pennsylvania, Compiled Marriages, 1700–1900' (col. 1502909), and the church collection (col. 1401638). If all return negative, Thomas likely married in Ireland before emigrating, and Irish Catholic parish registers should follow as a separate plan.

Ancestry indexes Pennsylvania church and religious records beyond what FamilySearch has transcribed, including additional Catholic sacramental registers from coal-region parishes. If pli_007 (FamilySearch church) yields no marriage or family record for Thomas Flynn, Ancestry provides independent coverage of the same underlying registers and may surface entries missed by the FamilySearch transcription. The locality guide notes some coal-region church films are browse-only on FamilySearch; Ancestry may have indexed those separately.

The FamilySearch search of Schuylkill County probate (pli_006, log_006) returned no record for Thomas Flynn. Ancestry indexes Pennsylvania probate independently and may surface entries not returned by FamilySearch — different transcription, different coverage window. Thomas Flynn's will or administration would directly name his children, providing primary evidence of the parent-child relationship with Patrick. The date range extends to 1910 because Thomas's death date is unknown; he may have survived well past 1900.

Patrick Flynn married sometime in the 1860–1908 gap identified in timeline t_001. Pennsylvania marriage records of this era — particularly church records and the county marriage registers indexed in 'Pennsylvania, County Marriages, 1885–1950' (col. 1921317) — sometimes name the parents of both parties, which would directly corroborate Thomas Flynn as Patrick's father and may finally identify Patrick's mother. Finding Patrick's marriage also anchors the gap in his life record and may reveal his wife's surname for further FAN-cluster research.

Cemetery and burial records for Thomas Flynn (death date unknown) and Patrick Flynn (d. 1908) may appear on FindAGrave or BillionGraves. Gravestone inscriptions in Catholic coal-region cemeteries sometimes name spouses and children. If Thomas Flynn is interred in the same cemetery as Patrick — common for Irish Catholic families — adjacent stones or a shared plot would corroborate the father-son relationship, give Thomas's death date (narrowing the probate search window), and may name Thomas's wife.

## FAN Research Plan — pl_003
**Question:** q_001 — Who were the parents of Patrick Flynn (b. ~1845, d. 1908)?
**Prior work:** pl_002 completed — 1860 census, death certificate, and Schuylkill County probate all done. Conclusion sits at *probable* only; the death cert informant (son-in-law James Brown) is secondary, both census sources give only indirect co-residence evidence, and **Patrick's mother has no name in any record yet**.

---

### The 9 Plan Items

| # | ID | Record Type | What it targets in FAN terms |
|---|-----|------------|-------------------------------|

| 2 | pli_008 | Census 1870 | Closes the decade gap; extends the unbroken co-residence chain; FAN cluster around Thomas's household. |
| 3 | pli_009 | Census 1900 | Patrick's own household — may contain an aged widowed mother (first record to name her). |
| 4 | pli_010 | Vital record (marriage) | **Thomas Flynn's marriage** — the single most direct path to the mother's maiden name, which is entirely unknown. FamilySearch PA Church & Town Records (coll. 1401638), Compiled Marriages (coll. 1502909), Civil Marriages (coll. 1571957). |
| 5 | pli_011 | Other (naturalization) | Thomas Flynn's naturalization papers — may name wife, list children with ages, and give the **Irish county/parish of origin** (unlocking a follow-on baptism record search). Browse-only films may require image sweeps per loc_001. |
| 6 | pli_012 | Land/deed records | Schuylkill County deed index — a deed "to my son Patrick Flynn" is direct evidence; a post-Thomas partition/heirs' release names *all* children. Deed witnesses = FAN associates. |
| 7 | pli_013 | Newspaper obituaries | Miners' Journal + Chronicling America — Patrick's 1908 notice and Thomas's death notice (est. 1870s–1890s) likely name parents/children. Siblings' obituaries are secondary targets. |
| 8 | pli_014 | Church records | FamilySearch PA Church & Town Records (1.2M records) — Patrick's confirmation or marriage names parents; Thomas's burial may list family. Fallback: Diocese of Philadelphia (pre-1868) and Diocese of Scranton archives. Some films are browse-only (loc_001). |
| 9 | pli_015 | Census neighborhood sweep | **FAN sweep** of Flynn households near Thomas's 1850 dwelling 84 / 1860 dwelling 112 — Irish immigrants clustered near kin; a nearby Patrick Sr. or John Flynn may be Thomas's sibling or parent, whose own death/probate/obituary records extend the cluster. |

---

### What this plan does that pl_002 didn't

- **Tackles the mother's complete absence** — items 4, 5, and 8 all have pathways to naming her
- **Pursues siblings as corroboration** — items 1, 7, and 9 all surface other Thomas Flynn children whose independent records can confirm the parentage link

entry.rationale: The 1880 census is the first U.S. enumeration to explicitly state household relationships. If Thomas Flynn (born ~1818, age ~62 in 1880) is still living and Patrick (born ~1845, age ~35) appears in or adjacent to his household with the relationship 'son,' that is direct evidence of parentage — upgrading the conclusion beyond the indirect co-residence inferences from pl_002. Even if Patrick has his own household by 1880, Thomas's household reveals other children (Patrick's potential siblings) whose death certificates, obituaries, and wills may independently corroborate the parentage. Gap explicitly flagged in ps_001.

The 1870 census bridges the decade gap between the last searched census (1860) and the relationship-stating 1880 census. Patrick (~25 in 1870) may still appear in Thomas Flynn's household, or in a nearby household with Thomas as a neighbor — extending the unbroken co-residence chain as indirect parentage evidence. Thomas (~52 in 1870) should be locatable in Schuylkill County. Other household members and near-neighbors may extend the FAN cluster. Gap explicitly flagged in ps_001.

By 1900 Patrick (~55) would head his own household; Thomas (~82, if still living) might appear there or with another child. The 1900 census asks for number of years married, mother's country of birth, and number of children born/surviving — data points that may independently corroborate the family structure. If Thomas is deceased by 1900, Patrick's household may still contain an aged widowed mother (Patrick's mother, whose name is currently unknown), which would be the first record to surface her identity. Gap flagged in ps_001.

Patrick's mother's name is entirely absent from every record examined so far — no source in the project names her. Thomas Flynn's marriage is the record most likely to supply her maiden name, confirm the marital unit, and (by the date of marriage relative to Patrick's ~1845 birth) corroborate the family structure. Search FamilySearch Pennsylvania Church and Town Records (coll. 1401638, 1.2M records, 1708-1985), Pennsylvania Compiled Marriages 1700-1900 (coll. 1502909), and Pennsylvania Civil Marriages 1677-1950 (coll. 1571957). Pre-1906 PA had no civil vital registration, so the marriage is most likely in a Catholic parish register — per loc_001, pre-1868 parishes fell under the Diocese of Philadelphia. If Thomas married in Ireland before emigrating, no PA record will exist; note that absence and plan an Irish church record follow-up if naturalization (sequence 5) identifies the origin parish.

Naturalization declarations and petitions are among the richest FAN documents for Irish immigrants: they typically name the petitioner's county and parish of origin in Ireland, wife's name, and children's names with ages at filing. Thomas Flynn's naturalization papers could supply (a) the mother's name — the single largest evidentiary gap for q_001 — (b) a list of children that includes Patrick, constituting direct evidence of parentage, and (c) the Irish origin needed for a follow-on baptism record search. FamilySearch holds Pennsylvania and federal court naturalization records for the anthracite coal region; Ancestry has independent indexes (NARA RG 85 and federal court records). Per loc_001 quirks, some films for this region are browse-only and require image-by-image review. If indexed FamilySearch search is negative, plan a browse-only sweep.

Schuylkill County deed transactions (grantor/grantee index) are a FAN-cluster source: a deed conveying property 'to my son Patrick Flynn' is direct parentage evidence; a partition deed or heirs' release deed executed after Thomas's death would name all heirs — every surviving child — constituting an independent list corroborating or extending the siblings identified through census records. Deed witnesses and co-grantors in Thomas Flynn's transactions are frequently relatives or close associates whose own records extend the cluster. Deeds are also useful for establishing when Thomas died (last deed as grantor / first deed referencing his estate). FamilySearch may hold Schuylkill County deed images; Ancestry and the county Recorder of Deeds are fallback repositories if FamilySearch coverage is thin.

Schuylkill County newspapers — chiefly the Miners' Journal (Pottsville, est. 1825) — routinely published obituaries and death notices naming parents, children, and siblings. Patrick Flynn's 1908 death notice may name his parents (Thomas Flynn and unknown mother); Thomas Flynn's death notice (whenever he died, likely 1870s-1890s) should list surviving children and may name siblings of Thomas who are part of the FAN cluster. Obituaries of Patrick's potential siblings identified through the 1880/1870 census searches are secondary targets. Search Chronicling America (Library of Congress, free) and Ancestry Newspapers. The Miners' Journal in particular covered the anthracite coal communities where the Flynn family lived.

Catholic sacramental registers are the primary pre-1906 vital record substitute for this community (per loc_001: no PA civil registration before 1906). FamilySearch Pennsylvania Church and Town Records (coll. 1401638, 1.2M records, 1708-1985) indexes baptism, confirmation, marriage, and burial registers. Patrick Flynn's confirmation (~1856-1861) or marriage record would name his parents directly; Thomas Flynn's burial entry may list family members. Pre-1868 parishes fell under the Diocese of Philadelphia; post-1868 under the Diocese of Scranton — if FamilySearch coverage is incomplete, contact both diocesan archives for unindexed registers. Per loc_001 quirks, some coal-region church films are browse-only and require page-by-page review of image groups.

FAN neighborhood sweep: review all Flynn households enumerated near Thomas Flynn's dwelling (1850: dwelling 84; 1860: dwelling 112) in Schuylkill County. Irish immigrants frequently settled near extended family — a Patrick Flynn Sr., John Flynn, or Mary Flynn of the right age and Irish birth in adjacent dwellings may be Thomas's parent, sibling, or in-law. These kinspeople are FAN associates whose own death certificates, wills, probate records, and obituaries may reference Thomas and his children, independently corroborating the parentage link for q_001. This is a targeted neighborhood sweep using the same FamilySearch census collection as log_001 and log_004 — not a repeat of the primary Patrick Flynn search but a systematic review of the surrounding enumeration pages.

---

## Research Plan pl_003 — Post-1860 Federal Censuses
**Question:** Who were the parents of Patrick Flynn (b. ~1845, Ireland, d. 1908 Schuylkill Co.)?

---

### Step 1 — 1870 Census, Schuylkill County · FamilySearch `pli_007`
Search for **both Patrick Flynn (~25) and Thomas Flynn (~52)**. This is the first census after Patrick reached adulthood. If they remain co-resident, it adds a third decennial data point to the 1850/1860 co-residence pattern. FamilySearch (NARA M593), free, indexed nationally.

### Step 2 — 1880 Census, Schuylkill County · FamilySearch `pli_008`
⭐ **The most valuable census year.** The 1880 census introduced the explicit **relationship-to-head column** for the first time. If Patrick (~35) and Thomas (~62) appear in the same household, the relationship field would deliver *direct, stated* evidence of parentage — upgrading the conclusion from *Probable* toward *Proved*. Thomas at 62 is plausible as "Father" in Patrick's household. FamilySearch (NARA T9), free, indexed nationally.

### Step 3 — 1900 Census, Schuylkill County · FamilySearch `pli_009`
Search for **Patrick (~55)**. The 1900 census records **father's birthplace and mother's birthplace** for every person — Patrick's record would attest to his father's origin (expected: Ireland), directly corroborating the Thomas Flynn identification. Note: the 1890 census was destroyed by fire (1921) and is not planned. FamilySearch (NARA T623), free, indexed nationally.

---

### Step 4 — 1870 Census, Schuylkill County · Ancestry `pli_010`
*Fallback for Step 1.* Ancestry's independently keyed 1870 index — catches what FamilySearch's transcription may miss due to surname variants (Flyn, Flinn, Glynn) or page-skip errors.

### Step 5 — 1880 Census, Schuylkill County · Ancestry `pli_011`
*Fallback for Step 2.* Given the relationship column's unique evidential value, a negative FamilySearch result warrants an independent Ancestry check before concluding the subjects left Schuylkill County.

### Step 6 — 1900 Census, Schuylkill County · Ancestry `pli_012`
*Fallback for Step 3.* Ancestry's independent 1900 index, including phonetic matching, for the father's-birthplace columns.

### Step 7 — 1870–1880 Censuses, Pennsylvania statewide · FamilySearch `pli_013`
*Fallback for Step 5.* If county-level searches for both 1870 and 1880 come up empty, broaden to all of Pennsylvania. Either man could have relocated (Patrick to adjacent mining counties; Thomas to another child's household). Search Thomas Flynn (born ~1818, Ireland) statewide; the 1880 relationship column remains the key target regardless of county.

---

**Scope:** 7 searches | **Fallback chains:** 1870 FS → 1870 Ancestry → PA statewide; 1880 FS → 1880 Ancestry → PA statewide; 1900 FS → 1900 Ancestry

**Next step:** Ready to execute — would you like me to start with Step 1 (1870 census search on FamilySearch)?

entry.rationale: Patrick would be ~25 and Thomas ~52 in 1870 — the first census after Patrick reached adulthood and after the Civil War. Searching for both men in Schuylkill County establishes whether they remained there together. Thomas at ~52 is still likely an active head of household. Finding Patrick in Thomas's household, or the two enumerated in adjacent dwellings, adds a third decennial corroboration to the parent-child co-residence pattern established in 1850 and 1860. FamilySearch hosts the 1870 census as a free, fully indexed national collection (NARA microfilm M593); begin here.

The 1880 census is the single most valuable of the three: it was the first US federal census to record each person's explicit relationship to the head of household. If Patrick (~35) and Thomas (~62) appear in the same household, the relationship column would provide DIRECT evidence of their kinship — upgrading the parentage conclusion from indirect co-residence evidence to a stated relationship. Thomas at 62 could be enumerated as 'Father' in Patrick's household, or Patrick could still be in Thomas's household. Even if they head separate households, locating Thomas in 1880 independently extends his life timeline and narrows the search for his death or probate record. FamilySearch hosts the 1880 census as a free, fully indexed national collection (NARA microfilm T9).

---

## Research Plan `pl_002` — *Who were the parents of Patrick Flynn (b. ~1845, d. 1908)?* (`q_001`)

**Starting point:** The 1850 census placed Patrick (age 5) in Thomas Flynn's Schuylkill County household (h_001). That's indirect evidence only — co-enumeration without a stated relationship. Every item below pushes toward direct evidence of the parent-child link and toward identifying the candidate mother.

---

### Step-by-step items

| # | ID | Record type | Where | Period | Why |
|---|-----|-------------|-------|--------|-----|
| 1 | `pli_004` | **Death certificate** | FamilySearch (PA Death Certs, 1906–1966) | 1908 | PA registration began 1906 — Patrick's cert exists. The informant names both parents directly. Highest-priority source. |
| 2 | `pli_005` | Death certificate *(fallback for pli_004)* | Ancestry | 1908 | Independent transcription; cross-check if FamilySearch has a misspelling or illegible image. |
| 3 | `pli_006` | **1880 census** | FamilySearch | 1880 | First federal census to state the relationship to head. If Patrick (~35) is still in Thomas's household, "son" would be written explicitly. Even if not, Thomas's entry names the candidate mother. |
| 4 | `pli_007` | **1860 census** | FamilySearch | 1860 | Patrick (~15) most likely still with Thomas. Second co-enumeration + candidate mother's first name if she appears. |
| 5 | `pli_008` | **1870 census** | FamilySearch | 1870 | Third snapshot of Thomas's household. Tracks whether Patrick remained and who else is listed. |
| 6 | `pli_009` | **Probate — Thomas Flynn's will** | FamilySearch image group 007936749 *(browse-only, 0% indexed, 412 images, 1851–1930)* | 1860–1900 | A will naming Patrick as a son is direct parentage evidence. Must be browsed image-by-image; the locality guide confirms Schuylkill County Register of Wills records survive from 1811. |
| 7 | `pli_010` | **Thomas Flynn & wife — marriage record** | FamilySearch collection 1401638 + browse-only church image group 007936751 *(0% indexed, 1842–1895)* | 1835–1848 | **Required dedicated item** per BCG planning standards. Supplies the candidate mother's maiden name and corroborates Thomas and his wife as a couple before Patrick's ~1845 birth. If not found in Pennsylvania, an Irish church record search should follow. |
| 8 | `pli_011` | **Patrick Flynn — marriage record** | FamilySearch collection 1401638 + browse-only marriage image group 007936753 *(0% indexed, 1870–1910)* | 1865–1880 | Patrick's marriage record commonly names "son of [father] and [mother]," giving direct parentage evidence. PA civil marriage registration wasn't required before 1885, so a Catholic parish record is the primary target. |
| 9 | `pli_012` | **Naturalization — Thomas Flynn** | FamilySearch browse-only image group 007936750 *(0% indexed, 298 images, 1849–1915)* | 1849–1870 | Thomas's Declaration/Petition may name his wife. Also: passenger manifests for a Flynn family arriving from Ireland in the late 1840s could place Patrick in the household before the 1850 census. |
| 10 | `pli_013` | **Cemetery / FindAGrave** *(FAN cluster)* | FindAGrave, BillionGraves, parish sexton records | 1845–1920 | Adjacent burials confirm family groupings. A joint headstone for Thomas and his wife names the candidate mother. Use the death certificate (pli_004) to locate Patrick's burial, then search nearby Flynn graves. |

---

### Fallback relationship
- If **pli_004** (FamilySearch death cert) yields nothing → execute **pli_005** (Ancestry).
- All other items are primary; none yet has a planned fallback (add one if a search comes back empty due to a repository gap).

---

### Scope
10 searches total. Items 1–5 are fully indexed (fast). Items 6, 7, 9 require browse-only image navigation (slower, plan extra time). Item 10 is an external site lookup.

entry.rationale: Pennsylvania statewide death registration began 1 January 1906, so Patrick Flynn's 1908 death has a state certificate. FamilySearch 'Pennsylvania, Death Certificates, 1906-1966' is indexed. The informant — most likely a spouse, adult child, or close associate who knew Patrick personally — would name both his father and mother, making this the highest-priority source for direct parentage evidence. Start here: free, indexed, and the most direct path to the answer.

Ancestry 'Pennsylvania, U.S., Death Certificates, 1906-1966' is an independent index of the same state death records. If FamilySearch (pli_004) yields no match due to a misspelling or indexing gap, Ancestry's separate transcription provides a cross-check. Also search here if the FamilySearch image is illegible and Ancestry's capture is cleaner.

The 1880 U.S. census is the first federal enumeration to record each person's relationship to the head of household. Patrick (~35 in 1880) may still be in Thomas Flynn's household, in which case the relationship column would explicitly read 'son' — direct evidence of the parent-child relationship the 1850 census only implies. Even if Patrick has his own household by then, Thomas Flynn's 1880 entry names any remaining children and gives the candidate mother's first name. Free and fully indexed on FamilySearch.

Patrick (~15 in 1860) was most likely still living in Thomas Flynn's household. A second co-enumeration with Thomas strengthens the candidate parent-child relationship. If a wife appears alongside Thomas in 1860, her name and age help identify Patrick's candidate mother. Free and indexed on FamilySearch.

Patrick (~25 in 1870) may still be in Thomas's household or have left. Either way, Thomas Flynn's 1870 household composition provides a third co-enumeration data point and may show Patrick's candidate mother alongside Thomas. Searching in sequence after 1860 (pli_007) to build a chronological picture of the Flynn household. Free and indexed on FamilySearch.

Thomas Flynn (~32 in 1850) likely died between 1860 and 1900. If he left a will, it would name his heirs — including any sons named Patrick — providing direct evidence of the father-son relationship. The FamilySearch 'Pennsylvania, Probate Records, 1683-1994' collection (id 1999196) covers Schuylkill County Register of Wills records but has 0% record-searchable entries (confirmed by volume search, image group 007936749, 1851–1930, 412 images). Browse the film by date range; the locality guide confirms probate records survive from 1811.

Dedicated search for the marriage of Thomas Flynn to Patrick's candidate mother — a required plan item for any parentage question. This record supplies the mother's maiden name (which census and death records typically omit) and, by its date prior to Patrick's ~1845 birth, corroborates Thomas and his wife as an established couple. Search both the indexed 'Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708-1985' (collection 1401638) and the browse-only Schuylkill County church records image group 007936751 (1842–1895, 187 images, 0% searchable). The couple may have married in Ireland before emigrating; if no Pennsylvania record is found, an Irish church record search should be added as a follow-on.

Patrick's own marriage record commonly names him as 'son of [father] and [mother],' providing direct parentage evidence. Search both the indexed 'Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708-1985' (collection 1401638) and the browse-only Schuylkill County Marriage Records image group 007936753 (1870–1910, 94 images, 0% searchable). Pennsylvania did not require county-level civil marriage registration before 1885, so a Catholic parish record is the primary target for a marriage in the late 1860s–1870s.

Thomas Flynn immigrated from Ireland; his Declaration of Intention and/or Petition for Naturalization filed in Schuylkill County may name his wife, providing the candidate mother's identity. The browse-only naturalization records image group 007936750 (1849–1915, 298 images, 0% searchable) must be browsed. Also search FamilySearch passenger manifests for the late 1840s for a Flynn family arriving together — a manifest listing Thomas with wife and children would place Patrick in the household before the 1850 census.

FAN-cluster item. Adjacent burials in Schuylkill County Catholic cemeteries often confirm family groupings. Headstone inscriptions occasionally name relationships ('son of Thomas and [wife] Flynn'). A joint stone for Thomas and his wife would supply the candidate mother's name. Identify Patrick Flynn's burial location first (likely noted in the 1908 death certificate), then search FindAGrave and Billiongraves for nearby Flynn burials. Sexton records, if accessible at the parish, may add detail absent from headstones.

entry.rationale: Pennsylvania statewide death registration began 1 January 1906, so Patrick Flynn's 1908 death has a state certificate. FamilySearch 'Pennsylvania, Death Certificates, 1906-1966' is indexed. The informant — most likely a spouse, adult child, or close associate who knew Patrick personally — would name both his father and mother, making this the highest-priority source for direct parentage evidence. Start here: free, indexed, and the most direct path to the answer.

Ancestry 'Pennsylvania, U.S., Death Certificates, 1906-1966' is an independent index of the same state death records. If FamilySearch (pli_004) yields no match due to a misspelling or indexing gap, Ancestry's separate transcription provides a cross-check. Also search here if the FamilySearch image is illegible and Ancestry's capture is cleaner.

The 1880 U.S. census is the first federal enumeration to record each person's relationship to the head of household. Patrick (~35 in 1880) may still be in Thomas Flynn's household, in which case the relationship column would explicitly read 'son' — direct evidence of the parent-child relationship the 1850 census only implies. Even if Patrick has his own household by then, Thomas Flynn's 1880 entry names any remaining children and gives the candidate mother's first name. Free and fully indexed on FamilySearch.

Patrick (~15 in 1860) was most likely still living in Thomas Flynn's household. A second co-enumeration with Thomas strengthens the candidate parent-child relationship. If a wife appears alongside Thomas in 1860, her name and age help identify Patrick's candidate mother. Free and indexed on FamilySearch.

Patrick (~25 in 1870) may still be in Thomas's household or have left. Either way, Thomas Flynn's 1870 household composition provides a third co-enumeration data point and may show Patrick's candidate mother alongside Thomas. Searching in sequence after 1860 (pli_007) to build a chronological picture of the Flynn household. Free and indexed on FamilySearch.

Thomas Flynn (~32 in 1850) likely died between 1860 and 1900. If he left a will, it would name his heirs — including any sons named Patrick — providing direct evidence of the father-son relationship. The FamilySearch 'Pennsylvania, Probate Records, 1683-1994' collection (id 1999196) covers Schuylkill County Register of Wills records but has 0% record-searchable entries (confirmed by volume search, image group 007936749, 1851–1930, 412 images). Browse the film by date range; the locality guide confirms probate records survive from 1811.

Dedicated search for the marriage of Thomas Flynn to Patrick's candidate mother — a required plan item for any parentage question. This record supplies the mother's maiden name (which census and death records typically omit) and, by its date prior to Patrick's ~1845 birth, corroborates Thomas and his wife as an established couple. Search both the indexed 'Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708-1985' (collection 1401638) and the browse-only Schuylkill County church records image group 007936751 (1842–1895, 187 images, 0% searchable). The couple may have married in Ireland before emigrating; if no Pennsylvania record is found, an Irish church record search should be added as a follow-on.

Patrick's own marriage record commonly names him as 'son of [father] and [mother],' providing direct parentage evidence. Search both the indexed 'Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708-1985' (collection 1401638) and the browse-only Schuylkill County Marriage Records image group 007936753 (1870–1910, 94 images, 0% searchable). Pennsylvania did not require county-level civil marriage registration before 1885, so a Catholic parish record is the primary target for a marriage in the late 1860s–1870s.

Thomas Flynn immigrated from Ireland; his Declaration of Intention and/or Petition for Naturalization filed in Schuylkill County may name his wife, providing the candidate mother's identity. The browse-only naturalization records image group 007936750 (1849–1915, 298 images, 0% searchable) must be browsed. Also search FamilySearch passenger manifests for the late 1840s for a Flynn family arriving together — a manifest listing Thomas with wife and children would place Patrick in the household before the 1850 census.

FAN-cluster item. Adjacent burials in Schuylkill County Catholic cemeteries often confirm family groupings. Headstone inscriptions occasionally name relationships ('son of Thomas and [wife] Flynn'). A joint stone for Thomas and his wife would supply the candidate mother's name. Identify Patrick Flynn's burial location first (likely noted in the 1908 death certificate), then search FindAGrave and Billiongraves for nearby Flynn burials. Sexton records, if accessible at the parish, may add detail absent from headstones.

### Rationale highlights

**Items 1–3 (Census trifecta)** close the major 1860–1908 timeline gap flagged in `t_001`. The **1870 census** is the single highest-priority item: Thomas Flynn would be ~52, and the census lists his wife by name — the first real chance to identify Patrick's mother. The **1880 census** adds the relationship column and parental-birthplace columns; if Patrick is still in Thomas's household, the parent-child relationship is stated explicitly for the first time. The **1900 census** confirms identity continuity eight years before Patrick's death.

**Item 4 (Church — candidate-parents' marriage)** is a dedicated required item for parentage questions. Thomas and his wife's Catholic marriage record names her maiden name — the only record type that routinely does. The *Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records* collection (1.2M records) covers Schuylkill County parishes; the marriage and civil-marriage collections are parallel fallbacks. A baptismal entry for Patrick would be direct parentage evidence.

**Item 5 (Military)** targets Patrick's Civil War service. Pension files for Irish immigrants in this period regularly contain family depositions that corroborate parentage.

**Item 6 (Naturalization)** targets Thomas Flynn's court naturalization record, which may list wife and children; browse-only films noted by the locality guide apply here.

**Item 7 (Probate/Ancestry)** is the fallback for the negative FamilySearch probate search (`log_006`/`pli_006`). Ancestry's independent index may surface administration records for an intestate estate.

**Items 8–9 (Land + FAN)** round out topical breadth: a deed of gift or conveyance from Thomas to Patrick is direct parentage evidence; death certificates of Patrick's probable siblings (post-1906 Pennsylvania civil registration, FamilySearch collection 2255317) would name their parents and could identify the mother's name for the first time.

entry.rationale: Thomas Flynn (b. ~1818) would be approximately 52 in 1870. The 1870 census lists all household members by name including the wife — the first opportunity to identify Patrick's mother, whose name no source yet supplies. If Thomas was still alive and remained in Schuylkill County, this record names her. Patrick Flynn (~25) may also appear, either still in Thomas's household or in a nearby dwelling. Highest priority in this plan: the mother is entirely unidentified.

The 1880 census introduced (a) the explicit relationship column and (b) separate columns for each person's father's and mother's birthplace. Patrick Flynn (~35) as head of his own household would record Ireland for both parental birthplaces, corroborating Thomas's Irish origin. Thomas Flynn (~62) may still be alive; his household would name his wife. If Patrick still resided with Thomas, the relationship column would state it explicitly — the first such direct statement in any census. Closes the major 1860–1908 timeline gap documented in t_001.

Patrick Flynn (~55) should be traceable in 1900, eight years before his 1908 death. The 1900 census records birth month and year, birthplace, and parents' birthplaces. Locating him closes more of the 1860–1908 timeline gap and provides additional identity corroboration linking the 1860 household to the 1908 death certificate. His wife's name and children, if listed, expand the FAN cluster for further searching.

Dedicated candidate-parents-marriage item (required for any parentage question). Irish immigrants in Schuylkill County's anthracite region were predominantly Roman Catholic (Diocese of Philadelphia until 1868, then Diocese of Scranton). A marriage record for Thomas Flynn directly names his wife's maiden name — the only record type that routinely supplies it, which census and death records consistently omit. FamilySearch collection 'Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708–1985' (1.2M records) indexes Schuylkill County parish registers; also search 'Pennsylvania, Civil Marriages, 1677–1950' and 'Pennsylvania, Compiled Marriages, 1700–1900' as parallel indexed sources. A baptismal entry for Patrick would list both parents' names — direct evidence of parentage. Note: if the family was still in Ireland at Patrick's ~1845 birth, the marriage may predate immigration; this search targets any Pennsylvania church record for Thomas.

Patrick Flynn (b. ~1845) would have been 16–20 during the Civil War. Schuylkill County furnished multiple infantry regiments and many Irish immigrants enlisted. An enlistment record gives age and birthplace, confirming identity. A pension application filed years later often contains depositions with detailed family history, witnesses, and birthplace — pension files have corroborated parentage in comparable Irish immigrant cases. FamilySearch indexes the Union pension index and Pennsylvania draft registrations (1863, 1864). Civil War draft registrations list age, birthplace, and occupation; if Patrick registered, this provides an independent data point outside the Flynn household.

Irish immigrants in Schuylkill County naturalized through the county Court of Common Pleas. A declaration of intention or final naturalization paper for Thomas Flynn would record his birthplace, arrival date, age, and — in later forms — his wife's name and children. Even earlier naturalization records list witnesses who were often neighbors or kinsmen. This is direct evidence about Thomas and potentially the first record to name Patrick's mother. Per the locality guide, some coal-region naturalization films are browse-only image groups; plan to browse if the indexed search is negative.

The FamilySearch probate search (log_006, pli_006) returned no results for Thomas Flynn in Schuylkill County 1870–1900. Ancestry's 'Pennsylvania, Wills and Probate Records, 1683–1993' is independently indexed and may include administration records (intestate estate) not captured by the FamilySearch index. If Thomas died intestate, letters of administration would still name his widow and heirs, including Patrick — and naming Patrick as an heir is direct evidence of the parent-child relationship. A will directly naming Patrick as a son would advance the conclusion from 'probable' to 'proved.'

If Thomas Flynn owned property in Schuylkill County, the grantor/grantee deed index records his transactions. A deed of gift 'to my son Patrick Flynn' or a conveyance between Thomas and Patrick would be direct evidence of the parent-child relationship. Deeds transferring property among heirs after Thomas's death may list his children by name, providing the same evidentiary value as a probate record. FamilySearch holds partial coverage of Pennsylvania deed books; the Schuylkill County Recorder of Deeds holds originals.

---

## Research Plan pl_001

**Subject:** Mikkel Nielsen Blyeberg, b. 20 Aug 1779, Stagstrup Sogn, Thisted Amt, Denmark  
**Patronymic clue:** "Nielsen" = son of Niels — the father's given name is established; the full name is not.  
**Boundary note:** Pre-1793 the parish was under *Vestervig Amt*; the 1793 reform created Thisted Amt. Records run continuously across the change.

---

### Step 1 — pli_001 · Baptism record · FamilySearch
Browse **Stagstrup kirkebog, volume 004130616** (1763–1814, 486 images, only **3% indexed** — go straight to images). The 1779 baptism entry names both parents directly. This is the highest-probability, most direct source. Rigsarkivet Arkivalieronline also holds the same images free.

### Step 2 — pli_002 · 1787 Danish Census · FamilySearch
Search **collection 1401625** (indexed) for Mikkel (~age 8) or Niels in Stagstrup. The 1787 census lists household composition with names and relationships — should place Mikkel under his father. Free cross-check: Dansk Demografisk Database (ddd.dka.dk).

### Step 3 — pli_003 · Military Levy Rolls (Lægdsruller) · FamilySearch *(fallback for pli_001)*
Browse **volume 004748896** (Vestervig/Thisted Amt, 1789–1814, 1204 images, **0% indexed**). Every boy is enrolled under his father's name from childhood — Mikkel (~10 in 1789) appears directly beneath his father's entry. **Direct parentage evidence.** Also on Rigsarkivet Arkivalieronline free.

### Step 4 — pli_004 · Parents' Marriage Record · FamilySearch
Browse **Stagstrup kirkebog volume 004130616** for marriages 1765–1779. Dedicated item per BCG Standards for a parentage question — supplies the father's full patronymic and the mother's maiden name, corroborating whoever is named in the baptism or census.

### Step 5 — pli_005 · 1801 Danish Census · FamilySearch *(fallback for pli_002)*
Search **collection 1401625** for Mikkel (~age 22) in Stagstrup. If he's still in or near the father's household, this corroborates the relationship. Also on Dansk Demografisk Database.

### Step 6 — pli_006 · Probate Records (Skifteprotokoller) · FamilySearch *(fallback for pli_003)*
Browse **Hassing Herred probate volume 004392710** (1690–1850, 655 images, 0% indexed) for Niels as deceased after 1779. The estate settlement would name Mikkel as heir. Use probate index (collection 2015318) to narrow the year range first.

---

- Baptism (1) → Levy rolls (3) → Probate (6)
- 1787 Census (2) → 1801 Census (5)
- Parents' marriage (4) — standalone corroboration

entry.rationale: Pennsylvania began statewide civil death registration in 1906 (confirmed in loc_001). Patrick Flynn died in 1908, so a state death certificate must exist. Pennsylvania death certificates from this period include fields for the deceased's father's name, mother's maiden name, and both parents' birthplaces — potentially the single most direct and actionable evidence of parentage available. Search 'Pennsylvania Death Certificates, 1906-1966' on FamilySearch first (free, indexed).

Ancestry holds an independent index of Pennsylvania death certificates 1906-1966. If the FamilySearch search (pli_004) misses Patrick due to a transcription variant ('Flinn', 'Flyn', 'Flynne'), Ancestry's separate indexing may surface the record. Search second as a paid fallback.

Patrick (~age 15 in 1860) was almost certainly still living in Thomas Flynn's household. A second consecutive census co-enumeration in Thomas's household substantially strengthens hypothesis h_001 (Thomas as father) beyond the single 1850 co-residence in a_004. The 1860 census also lists all household members by name — the wife/mother will appear, providing her first name for the marriage record search (pli_011). Note: the starting-point assumption that Thomas is the father rests solely on indirect 1850 co-enumeration; this search tests that assumption across a second enumeration.

The 1880 U.S. census introduced 'Father's Birthplace' and 'Mother's Birthplace' columns for every enumerated person. Patrick (~age 35) likely headed his own household by 1880. His entry will state both parents' birth countries — indirect evidence of their origin that, combined with Thomas Flynn's known Ireland birthplace, either corroborates or complicates the parentage hypothesis. Also search for Thomas Flynn in 1880: if still living, his household may include adult children or grandchildren who help reconstruct family structure.

The 1900 census is Patrick's last surviving enumeration before his 1908 death and likewise includes father's and mother's birthplace columns. Consistent birthplace statements across 1880 and 1900 reinforce the parentage profile. The 1900 census also records exact birth month and year, refining the ~1845 birth estimate in assertion a_002, and lists years in the United States — useful for narrowing the emigration window and potential immigration record search.

Loc_001 confirms Schuylkill County probate records (wills, administrations) are held by the county Register of Wills from 1811 and are indexed on FamilySearch in the 'Pennsylvania Probate Records, 1683-1994' collection (coll. 1999196). Thomas Flynn likely died sometime after 1850; a will naming Patrick Flynn as a son is direct evidence of parentage, and an administration proceeding listing heirs serves the same function. This is one of the strongest possible corroborating sources for hypothesis h_001.

Volume search identified a browse-only Schuylkill County Probate Records image group (DGS 007936749, 1851-1930, 412 images, 0% record-searchable). Loc_001 flags that some coal-region records are browse-only image series. If the indexed search in pli_009 returns no results for Thomas Flynn — likely given the 0% indexing rate — browse this image group directly, searching the name index pages at the front of the volume.

Per the record-type-guide for parentage questions, a dedicated plan item for the parents' marriage is required in every parentage plan. Thomas Flynn and his wife likely married before Patrick's birth (~1845) or shortly after emigrating to Pennsylvania. A marriage record: (a) identifies the wife as Patrick's mother by name including maiden name — information absent from census and death records; (b) by its date relative to Patrick's birth corroborates Thomas as father even where that fact rests only on indirect census co-residence; and (c) together with the other evidence, supplies a second corroborating link in the parentage chain. Search the indexed 'Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708-1985' (coll. 1401638) on FamilySearch. The Irish community in the anthracite coal region was predominantly Roman Catholic; target RC parish registers for Pottsville and Minersville. CAUTION: the couple's marriage alone does not prove Patrick is their child — a record placing Patrick with them is still required for a GPS-standard conclusion.

Volume search identified a browse-only Schuylkill County Church Records image group (DGS 007936751, 1842-1895, 187 images, 0% searchable, in English and German) and a separate Marriage Records image group (DGS 007936753, 1870-1910, 94 images, 0% searchable). Loc_001 notes RC parishes fell under the Diocese of Philadelphia until 1868. If the indexed church records search (pli_011) returns no Thomas Flynn marriage, browse these image groups directly — the marriage group covers 1870-1910 (useful for a second marriage if the first wife died) and the church group covers the earlier 1842-1895 period.

Patrick was born in Ireland ~1845 but was in Schuylkill County by 1850 (age 5). If the family emigrated with an infant, Patrick may have been baptized in a Schuylkill County Catholic parish after arrival. A baptism record naming the parents is direct evidence of parentage. Search the 'Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records' collection (coll. 1401638) for Patrick Flynn christening/baptism entries. Also browse the Schuylkill church records image group (DGS 007936751). Note: if searches here are negative, a future plan item for Irish baptism registers may be warranted.

BCG Standard 14 requires searching records of associates and relatives, not just the subject. Census neighbors enumerated in dwellings adjacent to Thomas Flynn's household (identified from the 1860 and 1870 censuses) may be siblings, parents, or in-laws — other Flynn households or households with the mother's maiden surname. Identifying Thomas Flynn's siblings or parents would corroborate Patrick's parentage through collateral evidence and may resolve the unknown mother's maiden name. After completing pli_006, search for Flynn households in nearby dwelling numbers and note any other Irish families enumerated alongside Thomas.
